title: Midmark Ritter 204 / 222 Series Exam Table - Hi-lo actuator not moving or intermittent
description: Table not raising/lowering or intermittent hi-lo movement due to power, hand control, or connection-related external issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove table from patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: Unexpected table movement or failure can cause injury.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Operate the hi-lo function using the hand control or foot control. Expected: No movement, delayed response, or intermittent operation. Why it matters: Confirms a true actuator issue vs user operation error.

steps.title: Check Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the table is plugged into a working outlet and power cord is intact. Test outlet with another device. Expected: Reliable power present. Why it matters: Loss of power is the most common cause of no movement. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Strain Relief
steps.instructions: Check for cuts, fraying, or loose connections at the plug and table entry point. Expected: Cord intact with no damage. Why it matters: Intermittent power can mimic actuator failure. If damaged → remove from service.

steps.title: Check Hand Control / Foot Control Function
steps.instructions: Inspect control for stuck buttons, physical damage, or inconsistent response. Try all movement buttons. Expected: Buttons respond consistently. Why it matters: Faulty controls are a common cause of intermittent movement. If available → swap with a known good control and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Control Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Verify that the hand/foot control cable is securely connected to the table with no bent pins or loose fit. Expected: Firm, secure connection. Why it matters: Loose or damaged connectors interrupt control signals. If connection is loose or damaged → reseat or remove from service.

steps.title: Observe for Actuator Noise or Attempted Movement
steps.instructions: Activate hi-lo and listen for motor noise or slight movement. Expected: Motor sound or movement attempt. Why it matters: Noise present → power/control reaching actuator. No noise → possible control or power issue.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect under the table for items blocking movement (cables, debris, storage items). Expected: Clear path for full actuator travel. Why it matters: Physical obstruction can stop or strain actuator movement. If obstruction found → remove and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Table Load and Weight Distribution
steps.instructions: Ensure table is not overloaded or unevenly loaded. Expected: Within normal operating weight and balanced. Why it matters: Excess load can prevent actuator movement or cause intermittent operation. If overloaded → reduce load and retest.

steps.title: Cycle Power to Reset System
steps.instructions: Unplug the table for 30 seconds, then reconnect and retest. Expected: Normal function restored if control fault was temporary. Why it matters: Resets control electronics and clears minor faults.

CCR.Complaint: Exam table not raising or lowering; intermittent response from hand control.
CCR.Cause: Hi-lo actuator unresponsive despite confirmed power and functional control.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent to Clinical Engineering for actuator evaluation and repair.

helpfulDetails: Audible actuator noise present or absent
Final device status (in service / out of service)

title: Midmark Ritter 204 / 222 Series Exam Table - Backrest not adjusting or drifting down
description: Troubleshooting guide for backrest not moving, drifting, or failing to hold position due to control, power, or mechanical issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: Backrest instability can cause patient injury or sudden repositioning.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Operate the backrest using the hand control. Observe for no movement, delayed response, or drifting after adjustment. Expected: Backrest fails to move or cannot hold position. Why it matters: Confirms whether issue is control-related or mechanical drift.

steps.title: Check Power to the Table
steps.instructions: Ensure the table is plugged into a working outlet and has power. Test outlet if needed. Expected: Table powers on consistently. Why it matters: Insufficient power can prevent actuator operation. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Function
steps.instructions: Test all buttons on the hand control, especially backrest up/down. Expected: Buttons respond consistently. Why it matters: A faulty control can mimic actuator failure. If buttons are unresponsive or inconsistent → replace hand control and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Cable and Connections
steps.instructions: Check for loose connections, pinched wiring, or visible damage along the cable and connection port. Expected: Secure connection with no damage. Why it matters: Intermittent signals can cause erratic or no movement. If damaged or loose → reseat or replace and retest.

steps.title: Listen for Actuator Response
steps.instructions: Press backrest controls and listen for motor noise from the actuator. Expected: Audible motor sound when activated. Why it matters: Helps distinguish electrical/control issues from mechanical failure. No sound: Possible control, wiring, or actuator failure. Sound but no movement: Likely mechanical issue or actuator slipping.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the backrest hinge area and linkage for debris, binding, or foreign objects. Expected: Smooth, unobstructed movement path. Why it matters: Obstruction can prevent movement or cause strain on actuator. If obstruction found → remove and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Backrest Support and Linkage
steps.instructions: Check pivot points, brackets, and mounting hardware for looseness or wear. Expected: All hardware secure with no excessive play. Why it matters: Worn or loose components can cause drifting or instability. If loose hardware → tighten and retest. If worn/damaged components → remove from service.

steps.title: Check for Hydraulic or Actuator Drift Behavior
steps.instructions: Raise the backrest and release the control. Observe if it slowly lowers on its own. Expected: Backrest holds position. Why it matters: Drifting typically indicates internal actuator or hydraulic failure. If drifting occurs → internal actuator failure likely.

CCR.Complaint: Backrest not adjusting and drifting down during use.
CCR.Cause: Failed backrest actuator causing inability to hold position.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for actuator replacement and repair.

title: Midmark Ritter 204 / 222 Series Exam Table - Hand control not responding
description: Hand control not activating table movement due to power, connection, or control-related external issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: Unexpected movement or loss of control can cause patient injury.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Press all buttons on the hand control and observe table response. Expected: No movement from any function or intermittent response. Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is total control failure or isolated to specific buttons.

steps.title: Check Power to the Table
steps.instructions: Ensure the table is plugged into a known working outlet and powered on. Test outlet if needed. Expected: Table has consistent power. Why it matters: No power = no control response. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Check where the hand control plugs into the table (usually under the seat or base). Ensure it is fully seated. Expected: Secure, tight connection with no looseness. Why it matters: Loose connections are a common cause of full control failure. If loose → reseat connection and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Cable Condition
steps.instructions: Trace the entire cable for cuts, pinching, kinks, or exposed wires. Pay close attention near strain relief points. Expected: Cable intact with no visible damage. Why it matters: Damaged cables interrupt signal transmission. If damaged → remove from service and replace hand control.

steps.title: Check for Pinched or Stretched Cable
steps.instructions: Look under the table and around moving parts where the cable may be caught or stretched during table movement. Expected: Cable moves freely without tension. Why it matters: Internal wire breakage often occurs from repeated pinching.

steps.title: Test Table with Alternate Control (if available)
steps.instructions: Swap with a known working hand control from another identical table. Expected: Table responds normally with alternate control. Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is the hand control or internal table electronics. If alternate works → replace defective hand control.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Response
steps.instructions: While pressing buttons, gently move the cable near connectors. Expected: No change in function. Why it matters: Intermittent operation indicates internal wire failure.

steps.title: Inspect for Signs of Liquid Intrusion or Damage
steps.instructions: Check the hand control housing for cracks, sticky buttons, or fluid exposure. Expected: Clean, dry, intact housing. Why it matters: Fluid intrusion commonly causes button failure. If contamination is present → replace hand control.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported hand control not responding; table would not move.
CCR.Cause: Hand control cable found damaged near strain relief causing loss of signal.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced hand control and verified full table functionality.

helpfulDetails: Signs of fluid exposure or physical damage
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Midmark Ritter 204 / 222 Series Exam Table - Paper Roll Holder or Headrest Mechanical Failure
description: Loose, broken, or non-functioning paper roll holder or headrest causing instability, inability to secure paper, or improper patient positioning.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: Headrest or support failure can lead to patient discomfort or injury.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Inspect the paper roll holder and headrest for looseness, detachment, or improper function. Expected: Identify whether issue is structural, alignment-related, or hardware-related. Why it matters: Confirms exact failure point before adjustments.

steps.title: Inspect Paper Roll Holder Mounting Points
steps.instructions: Check brackets, rods, and mounting screws securing the paper roll holder. Expected: All hardware present, aligned, and tight. Why it matters: Loose or missing hardware is the most common cause of holder failure. If loose → tighten and retest If missing/damaged → replace hardware or remove from service

steps.title: Check Paper Roll Rod Condition and Fit
steps.instructions: Remove and inspect the paper roll rod for bending, warping, or improper seating. Expected: Rod is straight and fits securely in holders. Why it matters: A bent rod prevents proper rotation and paper feeding. If damaged → replace rod

steps.title: Inspect Headrest Adjustment Mechanism
steps.instructions: Operate the headrest adjustment (manual lever, friction joint, or ratcheting mechanism). Expected: Smooth movement with ability to hold position firmly. Why it matters: Worn or slipping mechanisms lead to drifting or instability. If slipping → check for loose fasteners or worn friction components

steps.title: Check Headrest Mounting and Support Structure
steps.instructions: Inspect hinges, brackets, and attachment points between headrest and table frame. Expected: No excessive play, wobble, or separation. Why it matters: Structural looseness directly impacts patient support. If loose → tighten hardware If cracked or bent → remove from service

steps.title: Inspect Upholstery and Padding Integrity (Headrest Area)
steps.instructions: Check for torn upholstery, compressed padding, or exposed internal structure. Expected: Surface intact and supportive. Why it matters: Damage may indicate deeper structural issues or create infection control concerns. If damaged → send for upholstery repair or replacement

steps.title: Check for Interference or Misalignment
steps.instructions: Ensure no components are obstructing headrest movement or paper roll rotation (covers, accessories, debris). Expected: Full, unobstructed range of motion. Why it matters: Misalignment can mimic mechanical failure.

steps.title: Functional Test
steps.instructions: Reinstall paper roll and operate headrest through full range. Expected: Paper feeds smoothly and headrest holds position without drift. Why it matters: Confirms issue resolution under normal use conditions. If resolved → return to service

CCR.Complaint: Paper roll holder loose and headrest not holding position during patient exams.
CCR.Cause: Loose mounting hardware at headrest hinge and misaligned paper roll bracket.
CCR.Resolution: Tightened hardware, realigned components, and verified proper function.

helpfulDetails: Presence of bent rod or brackets
Final device status (returned to service or removed)

title: Midmark Ritter 204 / 222 Series Exam Table - Power supply or internal wiring faults
description: Troubleshooting power loss or intermittent operation caused by external power or connection-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: Power instability can lead to unexpected movement or loss of positioning.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to power on the table and operate all functions. Observe for no power, intermittent operation, or partial function. Expected: Confirm complete or intermittent loss of power. Why it matters: Determines if issue is total power failure or partial system issue.

steps.title: Check Power Source (Outlet)
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is plugged into a known working outlet. Test the outlet with another device if needed. Expected: Outlet provides consistent power. Why it matters: Power loss at the source is the most common and easiest issue to correct. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Check the entire power cord for cuts, fraying, kinks, or damage. Ensure plug blades are intact and secure. Expected: Cord is intact with no visible damage. Why it matters: Damaged cords can cause intermittent or no power. If damage is found → remove from service and replace cord.

steps.title: Check Power Cord Connection at Table
steps.instructions: Inspect where the power cord enters the table base. Ensure it is fully seated and not loose. Expected: Secure connection with no movement. Why it matters: Loose connections can mimic internal wiring faults. If loose → reseat and retest.

steps.title: Inspect for Strain or Pinching
steps.instructions: Check if the power cord is pinched under the base, wheels, or caught in moving parts. Expected: Cord is free and not under stress. Why it matters: Pinched wiring can cause intermittent power loss. If pinched → reposition cord and retest.

steps.title: Check Circuit Breaker or Power Switch (If Equipped)
steps.instructions: Locate any resettable breaker or main power switch on the table base. Reset if tripped. Expected: Breaker remains set and table powers on. Why it matters: Overcurrent conditions may trip protection and stop power. If breaker trips again → likely internal fault.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control and Control Cable
steps.instructions: Ensure the hand control is properly connected and cable is not damaged. Expected: Secure connection and intact cable. Why it matters: Some tables will appear non-functional if control signal is lost. If damaged or loose → correct and retest.

steps.title: Observe for Signs of Internal Electrical Issues
steps.instructions: Look for burning smell, unusual heat, clicking, or intermittent power behavior. Expected: No abnormal signs present. Why it matters: These indicate likely internal wiring or power supply failure. If any signs are present → remove from service immediately.

CCR.Complaint: Table would not power on and had no response to hand control.
CCR.Cause: No external power issues found; suspected internal power supply or wiring fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent to Clinical Engineering shop/vendor for repair.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual smell, heat, or sound
